metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what each detailed renderer (especially close errors and web ticket-review UI) displays when compute_validity fails, times out, or returns a partial/absent DerivedPlanReviewHealth object.
evidence: The plan enumerates drift, enforcement-off, legacy-unpinned, and missing-target states, but never addresses what a renderer displays when compute_validity returns an error, is unavailable, or yields a partial health object.
The close-errors surface is explicitly named as blocking user action ('close errors'), making a silent or uncaught compute_validity failure particularly high-stakes.

AC item: 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings' — implies a hard dependency on the contract being present and well-formed, but no fallback or degraded-mode is specified.
findings.finding: The plan specifies how renderers should display derived health in normal and drift states, but does not specify renderer behavior when compute_validity itself fails, times out, or returns partial/absent DerivedPlanReviewHealth data — leaving the close-error flow (which blocks a user action) and the web ticket-review UI without a defined error or degraded-data presentation path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'CLI show, MCP detail, audit text/JSON, close errors, and web review UI render the same derived health semantics'; Implementation: 'Renderers must not define independent status strings'
scenarios: A user attempts to close a ticket while the validity backend is temporarily unavailable; without a defined error state the renderer either crashes, shows stale/empty data, or silently succeeds — all of which are incorrect outcomes for a flow the plan marks as high-stakes.

The web ticket-review UI loads for a ticket whose compute_validity call times out; the plan provides no specified fallback presentation, so the UI could display an unhandled error or blank health section, confusing the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: 'When DerivedPlanReviewHealth is unavailable or compute_validity returns an error, renderers display a clearly labeled degraded/unavailable state rather than crashing or showing empty health data; the close-error flow must surface a human-readable failure reason and not silently unblock or block the close.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure posture and fallback DerivedPlanReviewHealth shape for all renderers when compute_validity fails, errors, or has no attestation to operate on (e.g., absent signature record in the audit path), consistent with the existing audit layer's best-effort/never-raise contract.
evidence: Existing audit path (src/rebar/audit/read.py, src/rebar/audit/page.py, src/rebar/audit/server.py) is designed as a pure sidecar-reader: best-effort, never-raise throughout.
compute_validity (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471) performs live I/O: file hashing, git reads, material fingerprint recomputation.
Plan says 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity' with no specified failure posture or fallback contract for the audit rendering context.
ABSENCE: No plan section addresses what DerivedPlanReviewHealth should contain when compute_validity raises or times out in the audit render path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces compute_validity calls into the audit render path (audit_trail → page.py → Jinja template), which is currently a pure sidecar-reader designed to be best-effort and never-raise. The plan says 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity' but does not specify how a compute_validity failure (I/O error, unverifiable signature, unreadable ticket state) integrates with this existing failure contract — leaving an unresolved edge case where a live-I/O failure could break the audit rendering posture.
findings.location: Implementation section / Acceptance Criteria: 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity'
scenarios: compute_validity fails (I/O error reading dep files or git) during an audit page render — does the page show an error, render degraded health, or propagate the exception?
The plan-review signature is unverifiable at audit render time — the plan currently provides no attestation to pass to compute_validity, since audit_trail reads sidecars rather than signatures.
A ticket has a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar but no SIGNATURE record — compute_validity has no attestation to operate on, yet the plan requires it to be called.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under Implementation specifying that a failed or unavailable compute_validity call (any exception, or no signature record to pass) must yield a sentinel DerivedPlanReviewHealth with a named 'unavailable' or 'no-attestation' pin_status, and that renderers must display this state gracefully rather than propagating the error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback protocol to the Implementation specifying renderer behavior when compute_validity fails or returns unexpected output (e.g., 'omit health section and log a warning', 'surface pin_status=unavailable'), consistent with the existing audit layer's best-effort contract.
evidence: compute_validity at attest.py:471 can return early with 'no certified plan-review attestation' or encounter I/O errors during material rehashing.
The plan's Implementation does not name a fallback rendering state (e.g., 'show unavailable', 'omit health section', 'surface error message') when compute_validity returns an error or raises.
AC line: 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity' — no corresponding failure mode is described.
The web audit page (src/rebar/audit/server.py) is documented as 'best-effort' — but the new plan requires it to call compute_validity, a change in reliability contract that is not addressed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies renderer behavior for the normal and drift cases, but does not specify the fallback when compute_validity itself fails, raises an exception, or returns an unexpected shape at render time. The audit, CLI show, MCP detail, close errors, and web review UI all call compute_validity, but no failure protocol is defined for any of them.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity'; Verification: 'Run focused audit, rendering, MCP schema, and CLI golden tests.'
scenarios: compute_validity raises during audit page render (e.g., repo_root unavailable), crashing the web UI instead of degrading gracefully.
An MCP ticket-detail call returns a 500 error instead of a partial DerivedPlanReviewHealth with a null/error pin_status field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where in the audit stack (audit_trail(), build_context(), or a new audit layer) compute_validity is called, how its required parameters (attestation, ticket_state, repo_root) are threaded through, and how its potential failures are handled given audit_trail()'s best-effort/never-raises contract.
evidence: src/rebar/audit/read.py: audit_trail() composes sidecar readers (_completion_attestation, _completion_sidecar_record) — no call to compute_validity anywhere in audit/read.py or audit/page.py
compute_validity is defined at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471 and is called by claim/close paths, but not the audit path
The plan does not name which file or function should be modified to introduce this call.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity' (AC line 1), but the existing audit read layer (src/rebar/audit/read.py) does not call compute_validity at all — it only reads stored sidecar data via best-effort readers. The plan does not specify whether compute_validity should be called inside audit_trail(), inside build_context(), or as a new layer, making the AC unverifiable as stated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Audit rendering calls compute_validity and displays enabled pin invalidity.'
scenarios: An implementor reads the AC and adds compute_validity to audit_trail() — but compute_validity needs ticket_state and repo_root parameters, and audit_trail already calls show_ticket(); the threading is unspecified.
The AC could also be satisfied by reading a pre-computed health field from the sidecar rather than calling compute_validity live — but the plan says 'calls compute_validity', implying a live call.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'close-versus-claim freshness' and 'pin-enforcement toggles' to the Implementation documentation deliverable list so it fully matches what AC5 requires.
evidence: Implementation: 'Document plan-material-pin, phase metadata, rollout behavior, deletion repair, and validity profiles in advanced plan-review/attestation documentation only'
AC5: 'advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness'
ABSENCE: 'close-versus-claim freshness' and 'toggles' do not appear in the Implementation documentation list
findings.finding: The Implementation documentation deliverable list ('plan-material-pin, phase metadata, rollout behavior, deletion repair, and validity profiles') omits 'close-versus-claim freshness' and does not clearly surface 'toggles', yet AC5 requires advanced documentation to cover both. This creates a cross-section contradiction: AC5 mandates coverage that Implementation does not describe producing.
findings.location: Implementation (documentation bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC5
scenarios: An implementer writing the documentation deliverable follows the Implementation bullet and omits close-versus-claim freshness and pin-enforcement toggles, then fails the AC5 check at review time.
A reviewer checking Implementation completeness finds no described work for the close-versus-claim freshness documentation AC5 demands.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the documentation bullet to: 'Document plan-material-pin, phase metadata, rollout behavior, deletion repair, validity profiles, pin-enforcement toggles, and close-versus-claim freshness in advanced plan-review/attestation documentation only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/gates.py` (and/or `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`) to the file_impact list with a description of how close-error output will render DerivedPlanReviewHealth.
evidence: file_impact list contains no entry for src/rebar/_commands/gates.py or src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py
src/rebar/_commands/gates.py:113-115 calls llm.claim_gate_check and emits its error text
AC explicitly names 'close errors' as a required render surface
findings.finding: The 'close errors' render surface named in AC3 has no corresponding edit target in the file_impact list. Close-time plan-review errors surface through src/rebar/_commands/gates.py (claim_gate_check → compute_validity error text) and/or src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py (close-gate error output). Neither file is listed, yet the AC requires these surfaces to 'render the same derived health semantics with canonical IDs and actionable drift reasons.'
findings.location: AC: 'CLI show, MCP detail, audit text/JSON, close errors, and web review UI render the same derived health semantics'
scenarios: A close attempt on a ticket with drifted pins returns the existing error string (from compute_validity's 'reason' field) rather than the actionable, canonically-IDed drift reason the AC requires, because the close-error renderer was never updated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the stable schema changes (e.g., field additions before removals, a schema version indicator, or a documented deprecation window) so consumers are not silently broken and the change is reversible.
evidence: 'Update stable schemas/output models and their compatibility tests' — the adjective 'stable' implies existing consumers depend on these shapes.
The plan acknowledges keeping 'ordinary list output and stored ticket events unchanged' but makes no equivalent commitment or migration plan for the detailed-surface schemas being changed.
No expand-contract sequencing, no rollback plan, no schema-version bump, no deprecation window is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan modifies 'stable schemas/output models' across multiple surfaces (CLI, MCP, audit JSON, web UI) but provides no rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or version-bump strategy for consumers who depend on the current stable shapes.
findings.location: Implementation bullet: 'Update stable schemas/output models and their compatibility tests.' / AC: 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases...'
scenarios: A CI pipeline or external tool that parses CLI show JSON or MCP ticket-detail output breaks silently after deployment.
A rollback of the feature is needed but the schema has already been updated in production, leaving downstream consumers in an inconsistent state.
Golden tests are updated to the new shape, removing the only record of the prior stable contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt an expand-contract approach: (1) first ship additive fields alongside existing ones, (2) update consumers, (3) remove old fields in a follow-up. Alternatively, document a schema version field and a rollback runbook that restores prior output shapes if the deployment must be reverted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md entry documenting the DerivedPlanReviewHealth shared-contract pattern, its rationale (single source of truth for status strings across all render surfaces), and the rule that renderers must not define independent status strings.
evidence: 'Renderers must not define independent status strings' — a new system-wide contract is established
Plan mentions updating 'advanced plan-review/attestation documentation' but scopes it to user-facing feature docs, not an architectural record
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new shared cross-component contract (DerivedPlanReviewHealth) spanning CLI, MCP, audit, close-errors, and web UI, but captures no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry recording this architectural decision and its rationale.
findings.location: Implementation section / Acceptance Criteria — 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings'
scenarios: A future developer adds a new renderer surface and is unaware of the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract, recreating status strings independently — the very anti-pattern the plan guards against
The contract evolves and there is no architectural record of why it was designed as a single source of truth
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-derived-plan-review-health-contract.md` recording: (1) the decision to centralize plan-review health semantics in DerivedPlanReviewHealth, (2) the affected surfaces, (3) the enforcement rule (no independent status strings in renderers), and (4) how to extend it when adding new render surfaces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a rollout strategy for the multi-surface renderer changes — either a feature flag gating DerivedPlanReviewHealth consumption (allowing per-surface enable), or explicitly document that the change is additive/display-only with a trivial revert path and coordinate deploy ordering across CLI, MCP, and web surfaces.
evidence: Plan mentions 'rollout behavior' only in the context of documenting the pin-enforcement feature's own rollout semantics, not the deployment of this plan's changes
No feature flag, canary, or phased rollout is mentioned for the multi-surface renderer changes
No rollback procedure is stated beyond 'run make check'
Multiple deployed surfaces are changed simultaneously: 'CLI show, MCP detail, audit text/JSON, close errors, and web review UI'
findings.finding: The plan changes runtime behavior across deployed surfaces (CLI, MCP server, web UI, audit output, close errors) but specifies no staged rollout strategy, feature flag, or explicit rollback path for the deployment of these changes.
findings.location: Verification section / Implementation section
scenarios: A rendering defect in DerivedPlanReviewHealth consumption breaks audit output for all operators simultaneously with no quick disable path
The web UI and CLI ship in different deploys and operators see inconsistent health semantics during the transition window
findings.suggested_fix: If the change is purely additive display (no schema breaking changes), document explicitly that rollback = revert commit + redeploy (no data cleanup needed). If schemas change, add a compatibility flag or version the output schema so old consumers keep working during the rollout window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or augment 'CLI golden tests' with assertion-table / fixture-driven tests that assert specific rendered fields against known-correct expected values derived from the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract spec (not from observed renderer output), so an incorrect initial rendering fails rather than being locked in.
evidence: 'Run focused audit, rendering, MCP schema, and CLI golden tests' — the phrase 'CLI golden tests' implies snapshot-based assertion of rendered strings.
No independent oracle for the correctness of rendered field values (e.g. a declarative spec or fixture-assertion table) is mentioned alongside the golden tests.
AC states renderers must 'not define independent status strings' but golden tests validate string output without an independent source of truth for correctness.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly names 'CLI golden tests' as a verification mechanism for renderer output. Golden tests are a self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: they snapshot whatever the renderer produces at authoring time and assert future output matches. If the initial rendering of DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields (fingerprint status, enforcement advisory label, legacy-unpinned distinction, phase metadata) is incorrect at the time tests are written, the golden tests will lock in that incorrect output rather than catching it.
findings.location: Verification section — 'Run focused audit, rendering, MCP schema, and CLI golden tests.'
scenarios: A developer authors the renderer incorrectly (e.g. displays 'advisory' label when enforcement is enabled, or omits the pinned fingerprint), writes the golden file from that wrong output, and all subsequent CI runs pass — locking in the bug.
The DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract changes a field name; golden tests catch the regression but cannot verify the new rendering is semantically correct.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a fixture table mapping each health profile (current, drifted-enforcement-on, drifted-enforcement-off, legacy-unpinned, missing-target) to expected rendered strings for each canonical field. Assert against this table rather than (or in addition to) a golden snapshot, so the oracle is the spec, not the current renderer output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that src/rebar/audit/page.py already defines independent SERIES/SERIES_LABEL status strings, and explicitly specify whether this pattern should be migrated to a shared contract, exempted (different domain), or left as-is — so the AC 'no recreating status strings' can be precisely scoped.
evidence: src/rebar/audit/page.py:18: SERIES: tuple[str, ...] = ("block", "advisory", "dropped", "indeterminate")
src/rebar/audit/page.py:19-24: SERIES_LABEL: dict[str, str] = {'block': 'Blocking', 'advisory': 'Advisory', ...}
These are status strings defined independently in the renderer, exactly what the plan forbids.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Renderers must not define independent status strings,' but the existing audit page renderer (src/rebar/audit/page.py lines 18-24) already defines its own independent SERIES and SERIES_LABEL status string vocabulary — directly contradicting this premise. The plan does not acknowledge this existing pattern or specify how/whether to migrate it.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Renderers must not define independent status strings.' / AC: 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings.'
scenarios: Implementors adding DerivedPlanReviewHealth consumption may not realize the existing page.py SERIES/SERIES_LABEL strings need to be reconciled — creating a split where some strings come from the contract and others are still locally defined.

A reviewer checking AC compliance may pass page.py because it consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth for pin/phase health while still having independent strings for the existing decision-series — silently violating the AC's intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` to the file_impact list and describe how `show_ticket` in that file will call `compute_validity`/obtain `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` and include it in the MCP tool response.
evidence: file_impact lists src/rebar/_mcp_models.py but not src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py
src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:48 show_ticket returns TicketStateOut.model_validate(rebar.show_ticket(ticket_id)) with no compute_validity call
src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:19-33 imports only model names from _mcp_models, no attest imports
findings.finding: src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py is an unenumerated required edit target. The MCP `show_ticket` tool is registered in _mcp_reads.py and currently returns `TicketStateOut.model_validate(rebar.show_ticket(ticket_id))`. To surface DerivedPlanReviewHealth in MCP ticket detail, _mcp_reads.py must be edited to call compute_validity and inject the health result. Adding a schema model in _mcp_models.py alone does not wire the data into the tool response.
findings.location: Implementation section / file_impact list / AC: 'MCP detail ... render the same derived health semantics'
scenarios: An implementer edits only _mcp_models.py to add a DerivedPlanReviewHealthOut class, ships it, and the MCP show_ticket tool still returns no health data because _mcp_reads.py was never updated to call compute_validity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/audit_trail.schema.json` to the file_impact list and explicitly state how DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields will be reflected in the audit trail schema.
evidence: src/rebar/schemas/audit_trail.schema.json exists and defines the `rebar audit show --output json` contract
Current audit_trail.schema.json has no DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields
file_impact list does not include src/rebar/schemas/audit_trail.schema.json
AC5 says 'Output schemas' must cover pins, phases, toggles, etc.
findings.finding: audit_trail.schema.json is an unenumerated schema that must be updated. If the audit JSON output (from `rebar audit show --output json`) is extended to include DerivedPlanReviewHealth, the existing src/rebar/schemas/audit_trail.schema.json schema must be updated, yet it is absent from the file_impact list. The plan's AC says 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness.'
findings.location: AC5: 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness'
scenarios: An implementer updates audit/read.py to populate health data but does not update the JSON schema; compatibility tests that pin the audit_trail schema structure either fail unexpectedly or miss the new fields entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/audit/test_rendering.py -k pin_health` or `make test-rendering`) per AC in the Verification section so each criterion maps to runnable evidence.
evidence: 'Run focused audit, rendering, MCP schema, and CLI golden tests' — no command given
'Exercise the web audit page with current, drifted, disabled, legacy, and missing-target fixtures' — described as an exercise, not a deterministic command
AC1 ('Audit rendering calls compute_validity') and AC3 ('CLI show, MCP detail... render the same derived health semantics') each need a distinct proving command
findings.finding: The Verification section names test categories ('focused audit, rendering, MCP schema, and CLI golden tests') but provides no concrete commands, file paths, or target names that an executing agent could run to produce pass/fail evidence for individual ACs — especially AC1, AC2, and AC3.
findings.location: Verification section
scenarios: An agent completes the implementation, runs `make check`, sees green, but the focused golden tests for audit/MCP were never wired — no proving command catches the gap.
A reviewer cannot confirm AC1 is satisfied without knowing which test file or target exercises compute_validity in the audit renderer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how AC2 will be enforced — e.g., a static import-graph assertion, a grep-based CI gate, or an architectural test that fails if any renderer module defines status strings outside DerivedPlanReviewHealth.
evidence: 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings' — no corresponding test strategy described
Verification section does not name a check that would detect an independent string constant in a renderer module
findings.finding: AC2 ('Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings') has no described proving mechanism — no test, lint rule, or import-graph check is specified that would catch a renderer independently defining status strings.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
scenarios: A renderer introduces a local STATUS_DRIFTED = 'drifted' constant. All golden tests pass because the string value is identical. The architectural constraint is violated but undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CI step such as: `grep -rn 'STATUS_\|"drifted"\|"advisory"' src/renderers/ | grep -v DerivedPlanReviewHealth` that fails on any independent status-string definition in renderer modules.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an automated end-to-end or snapshot test for the web audit page (covering at minimum drifted and disabled fixture states), or explicitly document why a manual exercise suffices and where the attestation is recorded.
evidence: 'Exercise the web audit page with current, drifted, disabled, legacy, and missing-target fixtures' — imperative but not a deterministic command
AC3 includes 'web review UI render the same derived health semantics' as a completion claim
No mention of a Playwright, Cypress, or snapshot test for the web surface
findings.finding: The web audit page verification is described as a manual exercise ('Exercise the web audit page with current, drifted, disabled, legacy, and missing-target fixtures') with no automated end-to-end check and no stated rationale for the absence of automation, which E6(e) requires.
findings.location: Verification section / AC3
scenarios: The web UI renderer introduces a separate status-string path that differs from CLI/MCP. Manual fixture exercise is done once, passes visually, but no regression catches a later regression or the initial divergence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Concretize AC4 by naming the observable indicator for 'advisory' and 'distinguishable' states (e.g., 'rendered as `[advisory]` label in CLI output and `"enforcement": "advisory"` in JSON schema') so golden tests can assert exact values.
evidence: 'Enforcement-off drift is visibly advisory, legacy-unpinned is distinguishable' — 'visibly' and 'distinguishable' are subjective without a named indicator
No golden-output excerpt or schema field is referenced to anchor what 'advisory' looks like
findings.finding: AC4 uses 'visibly advisory' and 'distinguishable' as the sole observable indicators for enforcement-off drift and legacy-unpinned states, without specifying the concrete UI/output element (e.g., a label text, a badge class, a JSON field value) that constitutes compliance — making the criterion subjectively evaluable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
scenarios: One implementer renders a grey italic label; another renders a parenthetical note. Both claim AC4 is satisfied. A reviewer cannot arbitrate without a concrete reference.
Golden tests for this AC require authors to decide what the expected output is — the plan defers that decision to the executor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC5 as specific observable outcomes, e.g., 'Schema includes a `pin_status` field validated by a compatibility test; advanced docs contain a section for each of: plan-material-pin, phase metadata, rollout behavior, deletion repair, validity profiles, and close-versus-claim freshness.'
evidence: 'cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness' — 'cover' is undefined
No minimum cardinality or observable check stated (e.g., 'a schema field exists for X', 'a doc section titled Y exists')
findings.finding: AC5 frames its criterion as coverage ('Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness') — this is effort/artifact framing, not a specific observable system outcome, and 'cover' has no stated minimum (one test per topic? a dedicated section?).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: An executor adds a single sentence mentioning 'pins' in an existing doc and a single test that touches a pin field, then marks AC5 complete. Another executor writes exhaustive dedicated sections. Both can claim compliance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'as applicable' with a concrete field-to-surface mapping (even a compact table) specifying which of the listed fields appears in each named renderer, or state a default rule (e.g., 'all fields in all detail surfaces; omit execution floor from error messages').
evidence: 'as applicable' — which fields are applicable to which renderer (CLI show vs MCP detail vs web UI vs audit JSON vs close errors) is not specified
No table or mapping exists in the plan that assigns fields to surfaces
findings.finding: The Implementation bullet 'Display each canonical related ticket, normalized role, pinned/current fingerprint status, missing-target state, enforcement effect, signed review phase, required phase, and effective execution floor as applicable' uses 'as applicable' to defer the field-to-renderer mapping decision to the executor, creating an ambiguous scope boundary.
findings.location: Implementation section — Display bullet
scenarios: An executor omits 'effective execution floor' from CLI show as 'not applicable.' A reviewer expects it. The plan provides no ground truth to resolve the dispute.
Two executors make different field-inclusion decisions for MCP detail vs audit JSON, producing inconsistent surfaces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a close-versus-claim freshness fixture (or explicit test scenario) to the Verification section's fixture list so verification fully covers what AC5 requires.
evidence: Verification: 'Exercise the web audit page with current, drifted, disabled, legacy, and missing-target fixtures'
AC5: 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness'
ABSENCE: no close-versus-claim freshness fixture or test listed in Verification
findings.finding: The Verification section's test and fixture inventory does not mention close-versus-claim freshness, yet AC5 requires that schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover it. This is a cross-section contradiction between the Verification strategy and AC5.
findings.location: Verification section vs. Acceptance Criteria AC5
scenarios: An implementer runs the verification steps as written, passes them, but the close-versus-claim freshness rendering path is never exercised and a regression goes undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Append 'and close-versus-claim freshness' to the web audit page fixture list: '...missing-target, and close-versus-claim freshness fixtures.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add WCAG 2.1 AA done-definitions to the web review UI scope: keyboard navigation of drift details, screen-reader labels for health indicators, contrast ratio ≥ 4.5:1 for text, and no color-alone encoding of enforcement state.
evidence: Implementation: 'web ticket-review UI'
AC3: 'web review UI render the same derived health semantics with canonical IDs and actionable drift reasons'
ABSENCE: no WCAG, keyboard, screen-reader, contrast, or touch-target language anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new web ticket-review UI surface ('web ticket-review UI render the same derived health semantics') with no accessibility done-definitions: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, keyboard navigation, screen-reader labelling, contrast ratios, or touch-target sizing are not mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Implementation (renderer list) and Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: The web review UI is shipped rendering pin-drift warnings using color alone (e.g. red/green indicators) with no keyboard-accessible alternative, failing WCAG 2.1 AA 1.4.1 (Use of Color) and 2.1.1 (Keyboard).
A screen-reader user navigating the web audit page cannot access drift reason details because they are not announced or labelled.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or Implementation note: 'Web review UI health indicators must meet WCAG 2.1 AA: keyboard-navigable drift detail (2.1.1), non-color enforcement state encoding (1.4.1), ≥4.5:1 text contrast (1.4.3), and ARIA labels for dynamic health status (4.1.2).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Current behavior' section (or a brief Expected/Actual) that shows what a CLI show, audit output, or web review UI currently displays for a drifted-pin scenario, so the necessity of each new rendering element is grounded.
evidence: Goal says 'Make pin drift, enforcement posture, and review-phase compatibility understandable wherever operators inspect detailed review evidence' — asserts the need without showing the current output lacks this.
No section describes what a current CLI show, MCP detail, or audit output looks like today, nor what an operator fails to accomplish because the information is absent.
ABSENCE: no reproduction steps, no before/after comparison, no named incident or ticket where an operator was unable to diagnose a pin-drift issue.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding pin-health rendering to multiple surfaces and updating output schemas, but never demonstrates the necessity of these changes by showing the current behavior is insufficient — no current-state description, no Expected/Actual, no named defect or operator-visible failure caused by the gap.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation sections
scenarios: An implementer or reviewer cannot confirm the change is warranted rather than gold-plating; if current surfaces already surface some of this health data, the scope of additions is unclear.
Without a baseline, the 'keep ordinary list output unchanged' constraint has no anchor — it is unclear what 'unchanged' means relative to today.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Current state' paragraph to the Goal section, e.g.: 'Today, `pm show <ticket>` and the web review UI display validity status but omit pin fingerprints, enforcement posture, and phase compatibility details. An operator who encounters a close-time enforcement failure has no in-surface information about which relationship caused it or whether drift is advisory or blocking.' This anchors the necessity of every AC item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency guard: state that this plan's implementation is gated on the sibling validity story delivering DerivedPlanReviewHealth (with field-level contract detail), and either reference the specific parent/sibling ticket ID or provide a minimal stub interface so implementors can proceed in parallel.
evidence: Grep of entire src/ for 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth' returns zero matches
Grep of src/ for 'plan_review_health', 'pin_status', 'pinned_fingerprint', 'legacy-unpinned', 'enforce_plan_material_pins', 'phase_status' all return zero matches in implementation code
The plan names this as the sibling 'validity story's' contract, confirming it is not yet in the codebase
findings.finding: The plan treats 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth' as a deliverable contract from a sibling 'validity story' that renderers can consume, but no such symbol or contract exists anywhere in src/ — it is a forward-dependency on work not yet implemented. This is an unverifiable premise that, if the sibling story is delayed or changes shape, removes the entire basis for this plan's work.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI.'
scenarios: If this story is implemented before the validity story delivers DerivedPlanReviewHealth, implementors have no contract to consume and must invent their own — which violates the AC 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings'.
The dependency order is unstated: this child plan could be picked up before the parent contract exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies' section that names the sibling story ticket, lists the minimum DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields expected (canonical_id, pin_status, enforcement_posture, phase_status), and specifies which AC items are blocked until that contract ships.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale statement explaining why compute-on-read is preferred over compute-at-emit for DerivedPlanReviewHealth (e.g., pin state can change after signing so freshness at render time is required, or enforcement posture must reflect current config not the config at sign time).
evidence: Implementation says 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI' — compute-on-read implied throughout.

Implementation also says 'Keep ordinary list output and stored ticket events unchanged' and AC says 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' — explicitly ruling out compute-at-emit without stating why.
ABSENCE: No sentence in the plan explains why freshness (a compute-on-read advantage) outweighs the simplicity/reliability of compute-at-emit for this health data.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing compute-on-read (calling compute_validity live at each renderer) over compute-at-emit (embedding DerivedPlanReviewHealth into the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar at signing time and having renderers read it from the existing sidecar). Per G6, the absence of a positive rationale for the chosen approach is a finding.
findings.location: Implementation section, overall approach description
scenarios: An implementer questions why the plan adds live I/O to render paths instead of persisting health at emit time, and has no plan text to consult.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit access class (e.g., authenticated-with-roles / internal-only-with-rationale) for the MCP ticket-detail endpoint and the web ticket-review UI paths that expose DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields, and add at least one auth/access-posture assertion to the MCP schema and web audit-page verification steps.
evidence: Plan names 'MCP ticket detail' and 'web ticket-review UI' as explicit rendering targets, confirming network reachability (gate opens)
Plan never states an access level (authenticated / internal-only / public) for either MCP detail or web UI paths that expose the new health fields
New fields include 'enforcement effect', 'signed review phase', 'required phase', 'effective execution floor' — organizational policy metadata that warrants an explicit access declaration
Verification section references 'MCP schema' tests and 'web audit page' fixtures but no auth/access posture test
findings.finding: The plan adds new derived-health fields (pin drift reasons, enforcement posture, signed review phase, required phase, execution floor, canonical related-ticket IDs) to two boundary-crossing surfaces — the MCP ticket-detail endpoint and the web ticket-review UI — without declaring an access class (public-with-rationale / authenticated-with-roles / internal-only) for either surface on these new data paths.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract in … MCP ticket detail … and the web ticket-review UI'); AC line 'CLI show, MCP detail, audit text/JSON, close errors, and web review UI render the same derived health semantics'
scenarios: In a shared or multi-tenant deployment, an unauthenticated or lower-privilege caller queries the MCP ticket-detail endpoint and receives enforcement posture and review-phase policy metadata it should not see
A web session without sufficient privilege renders the derived health panel exposing drift reasons and execution-floor constraints for tickets the actor cannot act on
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add a sentence such as: 'The MCP ticket-detail and web review-UI surfaces that expose DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields are restricted to the same authentication and authorization class as the parent ticket-detail view (authenticated session / role X); no unauthenticated access is permitted.' Then add a corresponding verification step: 'Confirm MCP schema tests assert the health fields are absent on unauthenticated or unprivileged requests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a forward-reference table or inline stub in this plan listing at minimum the field names, types, and nullable/required status of DerivedPlanReviewHealth (e.g., `canonical_id: str`, `pin_status: Literal['current','stale-pin-drift','stale-pin-missing','malformed-pin','legacy-unpinned']`, `enforcement_posture: str`, `phase_status: str | None`, `required_phase: str | None`, `effective_floor: float | None`, `targets: list[TargetPinDetail]`) so renderers have an authoritative contract without requiring the sibling story.
evidence: Search of all src/ files for 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth' returns zero matches.
The plan states 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' without naming or co-locating that contract's field vocabulary.
AC line: 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings' — an implementer cannot comply without knowing what fields the struct has.

Display list in Implementation ('canonical related ticket, normalized role, pinned/current fingerprint status, missing-target state, enforcement effect, signed review phase, required phase, and effective execution floor') enumerates display fields but gives them no type binding.
findings.finding: DerivedPlanReviewHealth is the central schema this plan's renderers consume, but its field-level vocabulary (field names, types, and per-field allowed values) is never defined in this plan — it is deferred entirely to a sibling 'validity story'. No such symbol exists in src/ (confirmed by exhaustive search). Any renderer implementer has no authoritative type contract to code against.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Consume the validity story's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract'; AC: 'Every detailed renderer consumes DerivedPlanReviewHealth without recreating status strings.'
scenarios: A renderer implementer reads this plan alone and has no schema to key on — they must reverse-engineer the sibling story to find field names, producing exactly the status-string duplication the plan forbids.
A schema compatibility test (AC: 'Update stable schemas/output models and their compatibility tests') cannot be written without knowing the field names and types.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Contract reference' subsection to Implementation that lists all DerivedPlanReviewHealth fields with their types and which sibling story defines each, so that when the sibling is complete the interface is unambiguous. At minimum: canonical_id (str), pin_status (five-value enum), enforcement_posture (bool or enum), phase_status (str|None), required_phase (str|None), effective_floor (float|None), targets (list[TargetPinDetail]).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the pin_status enum's five values and their severity-ordering aggregation rule (e.g., malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > legacy-unpinned > current) in this plan's Implementation section alongside the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract reference.
evidence: Search for 'pin_status', 'stale-pin-drift', 'stale-pin-missing', 'malformed-pin', 'legacy-unpinned' in src/ returns zero matches.
The plan's display requirements name 'pinned/current fingerprint status' and 'missing-target state' without specifying which enum values map to which renderer string.
The aggregation rule (which of the five statuses wins when multiple targets have mixed statuses) is absent from this plan and from the sibling-story cache artifacts reviewed in prior passes.
findings.finding: The five-value pin_status enum vocabulary (current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned) and the severity-ordering aggregation rule that reduces a list of per-target TargetPinDetail records to the single top-level DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status are named in this plan's display requirements but are not co-located with any schema or processing protocol definition. No such enum or aggregation rule exists in src/.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Display each canonical related ticket, normalized role, pinned/current fingerprint status, missing-target state, enforcement effect'; AC: 'Output schemas, rendering tests, and advanced documentation cover pins, phases, toggles, deleted targets, and close-versus-claim freshness.'
scenarios: A renderer displays 'stale-pin-drift' vs 'stale-pin-missing' inconsistently across CLI, MCP, and audit surfaces because each implementer infers the priority ordering independently.
The 'enforcement-off drift is visibly advisory' requirement cannot be implemented without knowing which enum value represents advisory-drift vs blocking-drift.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
